summary.leadingMessage: Recovery readiness is now a shared board-facing constraint and should be reinforced across multiple paths.

items.recoveryTheme: AI approvals move quickly, but the recovery path still depends on too few named fallback owners once policy and security collide.
items.boardQuestion: If the AI approval route breaks, who restores the path and how quickly can the board trust it again?
items.currentPosture: AI controls are visible, but fallback ownership is still too thin for a noisy exception cycle.
items.readinessHeadline: AI recovery readiness is weaker than the current board packet implies.
items.recoverySignal: The approval path reopens cleanly only when the same people are available to absorb policy and security collisions.
items.weakestFallback: Policy-review backup path during an AI exception burst
items.headline: AI needs named fallback ownership before the next board cycle depends on recovery.
items.narrative: The board should reinforce AI recovery ownership before another exception cycle turns one path failure into multiple approval stalls.
items.nextMove: Name a backup approval owner and test AI recovery under policy-review load.

handoffAssessment.message: Decision handoffs are stretching recovery and may soon slow the fallback path.

restoreAssessment.message: Recovery time is slipping and may soon strain the next board cycle.

escalationAssessment.message: Escalation gaps are now overwhelming the recovery path.

fallbackOwnerAssessment.message: Fallback ownership is too weak to support clean recovery.

redundancyAssessment.message: Path redundancy is too weak to support another break-and-recover cycle.

confidenceAssessment.message: Board confidence is becoming dependent on extra recovery explanation.

items.recoveryTheme: Identity can recover, but audit and security reviews still stretch the restore window longer than the board packet suggests.
items.boardQuestion: Which identity lane recovers too slowly once an exception route breaks?
items.currentPosture: Identity ownership exists, but restore time is still too dependent on review sequencing.
items.readinessHeadline: Identity recovery windows are too long for the next audit-driven cycle.
items.recoverySignal: The path stays open, but recovery time grows quickly once audit escalations queue behind security approvals.
items.weakestFallback: Audit exception restore path during stacked reviews
items.headline: Identity needs a shorter restore window before the next audit packet lands.
items.narrative: The board should shorten identity recovery time before another exception sequence stretches the restore path too far.
items.nextMove: Cut one duplicate review and pre-assign the recovery owner for audit exceptions.

handoffAssessment.message: Decision handoffs are stretching recovery and may soon slow the fallback path.

restoreAssessment.message: Recovery time is slipping and may soon strain the next board cycle.

escalationAssessment.message: Escalation gaps are now overwhelming the recovery path.

fallbackOwnerAssessment.message: Fallback ownership is too weak to support clean recovery.

redundancyAssessment.message: Path redundancy is too weak to support another break-and-recover cycle.

confidenceAssessment.message: Board confidence is too thin to trust this path after a break.

items.recoveryTheme: Revenue packets reopen because the restore path lacks enough redundancy when finance and commercial signoffs drift.
items.boardQuestion: If a forecast packet reopens, does the revenue lane have a second path to restore board trust quickly?
items.currentPosture: The revenue lane is measurable, but restore resilience remains incomplete across finance and commercial signoff.
items.readinessHeadline: Revenue recovery still depends on one narrow signoff route.
items.recoverySignal: The board packet shows who reviewed the decision, but not how the path recovers when one approver stalls.
items.weakestFallback: Commercial signoff backup during forecast reopen
items.headline: Revenue packets need redundancy before the next restore cycle starts.
items.narrative: The board should require a second restore path before another reopened forecast packet lands on finance.
items.nextMove: Add a backup signoff route and test revenue restore end to end.

handoffAssessment.message: Decision handoffs are too long to trust in a recovery scenario.

restoreAssessment.message: Recovery time is too long to trust before the next board-facing cycle.

escalationAssessment.message: Escalation gaps are now overwhelming the recovery path.

fallbackOwnerAssessment.message: Fallback ownership is too weak to support clean recovery.

redundancyAssessment.message: Path redundancy is too weak to support another break-and-recover cycle.

confidenceAssessment.message: Board confidence is too thin to trust this path after a break.

items.recoveryTheme: FinTech scope looks credible, but the restore path cannot absorb much more diligence load before fallback quality collapses.
items.boardQuestion: Which capital-sensitive lane lacks enough buffer to recover under simultaneous diligence pressure?
items.currentPosture: FinTech surfaces are credible, but the restore path is too fragile for another diligence burst.
items.readinessHeadline: FinTech recovery readiness is now a buffer-capacity problem.
items.recoverySignal: Merchant, treasury, and KYC reviews still converge on one overloaded recovery route.
items.weakestFallback: Parallel diligence restore path during merchant-risk and KYC spikes
items.headline: FinTech needs more recovery buffer before another scope expansion.
items.narrative: The board should buffer FinTech restore load before approving more work through the same diligence path.
items.nextMove: Add load buffers and a parallel restore route before the next diligence surge.

handoffAssessment.message: Decision handoffs are too long to trust in a recovery scenario.

restoreAssessment.message: Recovery time is too long to trust before the next board-facing cycle.

escalationAssessment.message: Escalation gaps are now overwhelming the recovery path.

fallbackOwnerAssessment.message: Fallback ownership is too weak to support clean recovery.

redundancyAssessment.message: Path redundancy is too weak to support another break-and-recover cycle.

confidenceAssessment.message: Board confidence is too thin to trust this path after a break.

items.recoveryTheme: Procurement remains the cleanest decision path and already behaves like the recovery reference model for the rest of the estate.
items.boardQuestion: Which lane already recovers cleanly enough to copy elsewhere?
items.currentPosture: Procurement closes cleanly because fallback ownership and restore playbooks are already built into the route.
items.readinessHeadline: Procurement remains the strongest recovery reference path in the estate.
items.recoverySignal: Trust and commercial packets close with enough redundancy and named fallback coverage to absorb more demand.
items.weakestFallback: None immediately visible under current modeled load
items.headline: Procurement shows the recovery pattern the rest of the estate should copy.
items.narrative: Use procurement as the reference lane for recovery ownership, redundancy, and restore confidence.
items.nextMove: Reuse the procurement recovery packet in one constrained adjacent lane.

handoffAssessment.message: Decision handoffs remain short enough to recover the path without extra coordination debt.

restoreAssessment.message: The path can be restored inside a board-safe recovery window.

escalationAssessment.message: Escalation gaps are low enough to keep recovery routing stable.

fallbackOwnerAssessment.message: Fallback ownership is strong enough to keep the path recoverable.

redundancyAssessment.message: Path redundancy is strong enough to absorb a recovery event.

confidenceAssessment.message: Board confidence remains strong enough to trust the recovery path.

items.recoveryTheme: Regulated control lanes still depend on too few durable restore paths instead of a stable fallback packet.
items.boardQuestion: Which regulated lane cannot recover cleanly if the board scales another control family?
items.currentPosture: Biotech controls are convincing, but restore readiness is still too distributed across quality, compliance, and operations.
items.readinessHeadline: Biotech recovery readiness is weaker than regulated packets suggest.
items.recoverySignal: CAPA, specimen, and safety packets still rely on narrow restore routes with weak fallback ownership.
items.weakestFallback: Quality and compliance restore path during the next regulated exception burst
items.headline: Biotech needs more recovery redundancy before more control-family expansion.
items.narrative: The board should require stronger regulated restore routes before scaling more control surfaces.
items.nextMove: Add one redundant regulated restore path and test it under exception load.

verification: Synthetic decision-path-recovery data only - no live board packets, actual committee routes, or real restoration logs are included.
Scores are modeled to show how Kinetic Gain can turn weak fallback ownership, thin redundancy, and long restore windows into board-readable recovery decisions.
All routes are read-only and demonstrate board-facing recovery readiness, not production workflow automation.
